mission.title: keylime: Set all string declarations using f-strings
mission.description: Set all string declarations using f-strings. Community-requested feature.

steps.title: Check current keylime deployment
steps.description:
Verify your keylime version and configuration:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n keylime -l app.kubernetes.io/name=keylime
helm list -n keylime 2>/dev/null || echo "Not installed via Helm"
```
This feature requires a working keylime installation.

steps.title: Review keylime configuration
steps.description:
Inspect the relevant keylime configuration:
```bash
kubectl get all -n keylime -l app.kubernetes.io/name=keylime
kubectl get configmap -n keylime -l app.kubernetes.io/part-of=keylime
```
Once Python 3 support is in, we should port string declaration to using f-strings.

f-strings are much easier to read. for example:

```
>>> name = "Eric"
>>> age = 74
>>> f"Hello, {name}. You are {age}."
'Hello, Eric.

steps.title: Apply the fix for Set all string declarations using f-strings
steps.description:
Just been thinking about this, the best approach (and easier for you) might be to backup your current repository / work `cp -r keylime keylime_fstrings`

Go into `keylime` and switch the master branch, grab the changes and merge them into your repo and push them:

`git fetch upstream`
`git merge
```yaml
>>> def to_lowercase(input):
...     return input.lower()

>>> name = "Eric Idle"
>>> f"{to_lowercase(name)} is funny."
'eric idle is funny.'
```

steps.title: Upgrade keylime to include the fix
steps.description:
If the fix is included in a newer release, upgrade keylime:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade keylime keylime/keylime --namespace keylime
```
Verify the upgrade:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n keylime
helm list -n keylime
```

steps.title: Verify the feature works
steps.description:
Test that the new capability is working as expected:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n keylime -l app.kubernetes.io/name=keylime
kubectl get events -n keylime --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' | tail -10
```
Confirm the feature described in "Set all string declarations using f-strings" is functioning correctly.

resolution.summary:
Just been thinking about this, the best approach (and easier for you) might be to backup your current repository / work `cp -r keylime keylime_fstrings`

Go into `keylime` and switch the master branch, grab the changes and merge them into your repo and push them:

`git fetch upstream`
`git merge upstream/master master`
`git push origin master`

After that create a new branch:

`git checkout -b
codeSnippets:
>>> def to_lowercase(input):
...     return input.lower()

>>> name = "Eric Idle"
>>> f"{to_lowercase(name)} is funny."
'eric idle is funny.'

[core]
	repositoryformatversion = 0
	filemode = true
	bare = false
	logallrefupdates = true
[remote "origin"]
	url = git@github.com:amylily1011/keylime.git
	fetch = +refs/heads/*:refs/remotes/origin/*
[branch "master"]
	remote = origin
	merge = refs/heads/master
[remote "upstream"]
	url = git://github.com/keylime/keylime.git
	fetch = +refs/heads/*:refs/remotes/upstream/*

prerequisites.description: A running Kubernetes cluster with keylime installed or the issue environment reproducible.
